 {"id":"bd-10i.11.3","title":"Implement input parser fuzzing","description":"# Implement input parser fuzzing

## Objective
Fuzz the InputParser to ensure it handles all possible byte sequences without panicking or excessive resource usage.

## Why Fuzzing?
- Terminal input is untrusted
- Malformed sequences are common
- DoS via crafted input is possible
- Parser must be robust

## Fuzz Targets

### Target 1: Raw Byte Parsing
fuzz_target\!(|data: &[u8]| {
    let mut parser = InputParser::new();
    let _ = parser.parse(data);
    // Must not panic
    // Must not hang
    // Must not exceed memory limits
});

### Target 2: Structured Fuzzing
// Generate semi-valid sequences
#[derive(Arbitrary)]
enum FuzzSequence {
    Char(u8),
    Escape,
    Csi(Vec<u8>),
    Osc(Vec<u8>),
    Mouse { button: u8, x: u16, y: u16 },
    Paste(Vec<u8>),
}

### Target 3: Long Sequences
// Specifically test DoS limits
fuzz_target\!(|len: u16| {
    let data = vec\![0x1b, b'[']  // CSI
        .into_iter()
        .chain(std::iter::repeat(b'0').take(len as usize))
        .chain(std::iter::once(b'm'))
        .collect::<Vec<_>>();

    let mut parser = InputParser::new();
    let _ = parser.parse(&data);
    // Should not allocate unbounded memory
});

## Properties to Verify
1. No panics on any input
2. Memory bounded (no OOM from crafted input)
3. Time bounded (no quadratic behavior)
4. Always returns to Ground state eventually
5. DoS limits enforced

## Corpus Seeds
- Valid key sequences
- Valid mouse sequences
- Bracketed paste boundaries
- OSC 8 links
- Malformed UTF-8
- Truncated sequences

## Running Fuzzer
cargo +nightly fuzz run input_parser -- -max_len=4096

## Integration with CI
- Run fuzzer for limited time on each PR
- Maintain corpus in repo
- Report new crashes immediately

## Tests Required
- [ ] Fuzz harness builds
- [ ] Initial corpus covers major cases
- [ ] No crashes in 1-hour fuzz session
- [ ] Memory limits enforced

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Fuzzer integrated in CI
- [ ] No panics found
- [ ] Corpus maintained

 {"id":"bd-10i.12","title":"Docs: Playbook, Glossary, References","description":"# Docs: Playbook, Glossary, References

## Purpose
Encode the *non-code* parts of PLAN_TO_CREATE_FRANKENTUI__OPUS.md into durable, discoverable docs so future work does **not** require rereading the plan document.

This epic is about:
- capturing the project's intent, constraints, and invariants
- preventing churn (ADRs + playbook)
- making the library *pleasant to adopt* (tutorials + examples)

## Scope (v1)
- Operational Playbook (Chapter 17)
- Terminal Compatibility Matrix (Chapter 16)
- Glossary (Appendix B)
- Risk Register (Appendix C)
- ANSI Reference extraction (Appendix A)
- Migration map / "what we kept" from the three libraries (Chapter 14 + 1.5)
- Public API docs + examples (Section 0.8)

## Non-goals
- Duplicating ADRs here (ADRs remain in bd-10i.10)
- Writing long-form tutorials before the kernel is stable

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Every doc deliverable in PLAN_TO_CREATE_FRANKENTUI__OPUS.md (Chapter 16/17 + Appendices A/B/C + Chapter 14) has a corresponding issue with acceptance criteria and test hooks where applicable.
- [ ] Docs are written with enough context that a contributor can implement features without reopening the original plan.
- [ ] Docs include explicit "undefined behavior" and "out of scope" notes where the plan is opinionated (one-writer rule, inline mode, sanitize-by-default).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:07:35.746628357Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-31T23:14:45.174558275Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-10i.12","depends_on_id":"bd-10i","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:07:35.746628357Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-10i.12","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.10","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:14:45.174515294Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-10i.12.1","title":"Write Operational Playbook (Chapter 17)","description":"# Write Operational Playbook (Chapter 17)

## Background
Chapter 17 exists to prevent the common failure mode of kernel projects: infinite refinement without a stable deliverable.

The playbook is part technical, part process:
- which PRs require which test gates
- how ADRs are written and frozen
- the recommended shipping order (avoid the widget trap)
- what "agent-ergonomic" means in concrete UX terms

## Deliverable
Create a durable doc (e.g. `docs/operational-playbook.md`) that captures:

### Merge-gate rules (from 17.1)
- Any change touching: inline mode cursor policy, presenter output sequencing, input parser, width measurement
  must include appropriate tests (unit/property/pty/snapshot) and must cite which invariants are preserved.

### ADR discipline (from 17.2)
- ADR template (Context / Decision / Alternatives / Consequences / Test Plan)
- Rules for when an ADR is required

### Minimal deliverables order (from 17.3)
1) Kernel (buffer/diff/presenter + terminal session + inline stability)
2) Runtime (update/view + simulator + snapshot)
3) Harness (reference app proving inline/log/UI)
4) Widgets (only harness essentials)
5) Extras (feature-gated)

### Agent-ergonomic checklist (from 17.4)
- Hello world harness in <200 LOC
- Tool output streaming is one function call
- Pinned bottom UI is one config option
- Temporary full-screen modal supported

### Immediate Next Steps checklist (from 17.5)
Convert the listed "first PRs" into checkboxes and link them to the relevant Beads tasks.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Doc exists with the sections above.
- [ ] Doc explicitly ties each rule to a reason (cursor safety, one-writer, determinism).
- [ ] Doc links to the relevant ADR issues and test-suite epics.
- [ ] Doc includes an "Out of scope" section to prevent accidental widget creep.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"docs","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:07:50.447594010Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T18:43:15.094260140Z","closed_at":"2026-02-01T18:43:15.094232978Z","close_reason":"Created docs/operational-playbook.md with all required sections: merge-gate rules, ADR discipline, deliverables order, agent-ergonomic checklist, immediate next steps, and out-of-scope section. Linked from README.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-10i.12.1","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.12","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:07:50.447594010Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

## Background
The plan includes a short glossary of terms that recur throughout the kernel design. This needs to be in-repo so future contributors don't need the plan doc to understand terminology.

## Deliverable
Create `docs/glossary.md` containing (at minimum) the glossary table below, plus any additional terms introduced by implementation.

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Cell** | Single grid position (content + fg + bg + attrs) |
| **Buffer** | 2D array of Cells representing display state |
| **Frame** | Buffer + metadata for a render pass |
| **Diff** | Set of (x, y) positions that changed between buffers |
| **Presenter** | State-tracked ANSI emitter |
| **Grapheme** | User-perceived character (may be multiple codepoints) |
| **GraphemePool** | Interned storage for complex grapheme clusters |
| **LinkRegistry** | URL storage for OSC 8 hyperlinks |
| **Scissor** | Clipping rectangle for rendering |
| **SGR** | Select Graphic Rendition (style codes) |
| **OSC** | Operating System Command (escape sequence) |
| **CSI** | Control Sequence Introducer (`\x1b[`) |
| **DEC** | Digital Equipment Corporation (terminal standard) |
| **ZWJ** | Zero Width Joiner (connects graphemes into compound) |
| **Porter-Duff** | Compositing algebra for alpha blending |

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `docs/glossary.md` exists.
- [ ] Table is present and correct.
- [ ] Any additional ftui-specific terms (e.g., TerminalWriter, ScreenMode, UiAnchor) are added with concise definitions.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"docs","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:08:02.312315920Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-31T23:08:02.312315920Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-10i.12.2","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.12","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:08:02.312315920Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-10i.12.3","title":"Write Risk Register + Mitigation Links (Appendix C)","description":"# Write Risk Register + Mitigation Links (Appendix C)

## Background
The plan includes a small Risk Register to keep engineering effort focused on the failure modes that destroy trust in a terminal UI kernel (cursor corruption, escape injection, width bugs, capability mismatches, unsafe creep, byte bloat, interleaved writes).

We want this risk list in-repo so:
- mitigations stay attached to code and tests
- adding features doesn't silently reintroduce known risks

## Deliverable
Create `docs/risk-register.md` that includes the plan's risks and extends them with:
- the *actual* Beads issue IDs that mitigate the risk
- the *actual* test suites / scripts that enforce the mitigation

## Required risks (from plan)
1) Inline mode cursor corruption
- Mitigation: PTY tests + strict cursor policy + centralized writer API for logs/UI.

1b) ANSI / OSC injection via untrusted log content
- Mitigation: sanitize-by-default + explicit opt-in raw passthrough + adversarial PTY tests.

2) Unicode width bugs
- Mitigation: curated test corpus + snapshot tests + ASCII fast path only when proven.

3) Terminal capability mismatches
- Mitigation: conservative defaults + feature detection + robust fallbacks.

4) Unsafe creep
- Mitigation: forbid unsafe by default; isolate any perf tricks; require tests + benches.

5) Presenter byte bloat
- Mitigation: run grouping + style-run coalescing + output-length benchmarks in CI.

6) Interleaved stdout writes from user code
- Mitigation: one-writer rule; TerminalSession/TerminalWriter; routing/capture patterns.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `docs/risk-register.md` exists and contains the risks above.
- [ ] Each mitigation links to specific Beads issues and/or specific test suites (unit/property/pty/e2e).
- [ ] The risk register includes a short section "How to add a new risk" and "When to revisit".","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"docs","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:08:14.246182Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T18:45:32.468683426Z","closed_at":"2026-02-01T18:45:32.468664770Z","close_reason":"Created docs/risk-register.md (339 lines) with 7 risks, mitigation links to beads/ADRs/tests, and How to Add/When to Revisit sections.  {"id":"bd-10i.12.4","title":"Extract ANSI Escape Reference into docs","description":"# Extract ANSI Escape Reference into docs

## Background
Appendix A of the plan contains a minimal ANSI escape sequence reference. Keeping this in-repo helps:
- implement Presenter/TerminalWriter correctly
- write terminal-model tests that parse the exact sequences we emit
- avoid "tribal knowledge" and copy/paste drift

## Deliverable
Create `docs/ansi-reference.md` with:
- SGR codes used by ftui
- Cursor movement + save/restore (ANSI + DEC)
- Screen control (alt screen, clear line, cursor show/hide)
- Synchronized output (DEC 2026)
- OSC 8 hyperlinks
- Mouse mode enables (SGR encoding)
- Bracketed paste enables + boundaries
- Focus events enables + events

Seed content (from plan):

### SGR
- `\x1b[0m` reset
- `\x1b[1m` bold
- `\x1b[3m` italic
- `\x1b[4m` underline
- `\x1b[38;2;R;G;Bm` truecolor fg
- `\x1b[48;2;R;G;Bm` truecolor bg
- `\x1b[38;5;Nm` 256 fg
- `\x1b[48;5;Nm` 256 bg

### Cursor
- `\x1b[{r};{c}H` move
- `\x1b[s` save (ANSI)
- `\x1b[u` restore (ANSI)
- `\x1b7` save (DEC)
- `\x1b8` restore (DEC)

### Synchronized Output
- `\x1b[?2026h` begin
- `\x1b[?2026l` end

### OSC 8
- `\x1b]8;;URL\x1b\\` start
- `\x1b]8;;\x1b\\` end

(Include the rest from Appendix A.)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `docs/ansi-reference.md` exists.
- [ ] Reference is sufficient to implement the presenter and terminal-model parser without consulting external docs.
- [ ] Doc explicitly notes which sequences are *emitted* vs merely *parsed*.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"docs","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:08:26.761368523Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T07:20:09.451560381Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-10i.12.4","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.12","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:08:26.761368523Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-10i.12.5","title":"Write Migration Map + Source→ftui mapping (Chapter 14 + 1.5)","description":"# Write Migration Map + Source→ftui mapping (Chapter 14 + 1.5)

## Background
The plan explicitly says FrankenTUI is *not* a 1:1 port. We keep concepts, not types.

However, future contributors will still ask:
- "Where did this idea come from?"
- "Which library handled X well?"
- "What did we intentionally drop?"

Chapter 14 (Migration Map) and Chapter 1.5 (What survives vs what gets dropped) answer that.

## Deliverable
Create `docs/migration-map.md` that includes:
- Migration principles (Chapter 14.1)
- Conceptual mapping: source concept → ftui concept (Chapter 14.2)
- Compatibility strategy notes (Chapter 14.3)
- Explicit "survives vs dropped" lists (Chapter 1.5)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Doc exists and is understandable without the plan.
- [ ] Doc includes a clear "Dropped / de-scoped" section to prevent accidental scope creep (widget zoo, full terminal emulation, etc.).

 {"id":"bd-10i.12.7","title":"Master TODO Inventory → Bead Map (Self-Contained Execution Tracker)","description":"# Master TODO Inventory → Bead Map (Self-Contained Execution Tracker)

## Why This Exists
The planning document (PLAN_TO_CREATE_FRANKENTUI__OPUS.md) is intentionally detailed, but once execution starts we want Beads to be the single source of truth.

This issue is the one place where we map the plan’s Master TODO Inventory (A–K) to concrete Beads IDs, so:
- we never need to re-open the plan document to remember what we intended
- we can quickly confirm completeness
- we can see the dependency structure at a glance

## How To Use This Issue
- When a new requirement is discovered, add it here and create/update the relevant bead(s).
- When a bead is renamed/re-scoped, update the mapping here.
- This is a checklist; the actual work happens in the referenced beads.

---

## A) Project + Docs
- [ ] Definition of Done (Ship Checklist) → bd-tb84
- [ ] Track Quality Gates (v1 Stop-Ship Criteria) → bd-2gx
- [ ] ADR discipline (locked decisions) → bd-10i.10 + children (bd-10i.10.1..bd-10i.10.6)
- [ ] Operational Playbook (merge gates, order of deliverables) → bd-10i.12.1
- [ ] Glossary (Appendix B) → bd-10i.12.2
- [ ] Risk Register (Appendix C) → bd-10i.12.3
- [ ] ANSI escape reference (Appendix A) → bd-10i.12.4
- [ ] Migration map (Chapter 14) → bd-10i.12.5
- [ ] One-writer rule user guidance → bd-10i.12.6
- [ ] Inline vs alt-screen tradeoffs → bd-1u5
- [ ] Windows v1 limitations → bd-2ss + ADR-004 bd-10i.10.4
- [ ] Terminal compatibility matrix (Chapter 16) → bd-1un
- [ ] Agent harness tutorial → bd-wo2

## B) Contracts (Public API + Kernel Boundaries)
- [ ] Workspace crate layout → bd-10i.2.1
- [ ] ftui facade crate (prelude + stable API) → bd-10i.2.7
- [ ] Define Cell API contract → bd-10i.2.2
- [ ] Define Buffer API contract → bd-10i.2.3
- [ ] Define Event types contract → bd-10i.2.4
- [ ] Define TerminalCapabilities contract (+ mux flags) → bd-10i.2.5
- [ ] Define TerminalSession lifecycle contract → bd-10i.2.6

## C) Rendering Engine (Kernel)
- [ ] CellContent encoding → bd-10i.3.1
- [ ] PackedRgba blending (Porter-Duff) → bd-10i.3.2
- [ ] CellAttrs + style flags → bd-10i.3.3
- [ ] 16-byte Cell struct → bd-10i.3.4
- [ ] GraphemeId encoding (slot+width packing) → bd-rk95
- [ ] GraphemePool refcounting → bd-10i.3.5
- [ ] Buffer (flat Vec, scissor, opacity, continuation cells) → bd-10i.3.6
- [ ] Rect/Sides/Measurement primitives → bd-10i.3.7
- [ ] Frame struct → bd-3cc
- [ ] Diff engine (row-major scan) → bd-10i.4.1
- [ ] Run grouping + style-run coalescing → bd-2yu

 {"id":"bd-2wo","title":"Implement tmux/screen passthrough and mux detection","description":"# Implement tmux/screen Passthrough and Mux Detection

## Background
Plan Section 9.4 and ADR-001 describe multiplexer (tmux/screen/zellij) detection and passthrough handling. This is important for inline mode to work correctly under multiplexers.

## Why This Matters
- tmux/screen intercept escape sequences
- Some features (sync output, OSC 8) need passthrough
- Scroll regions behave differently under muxes
- Detection enables correct fallback strategies

## Technical Design

### Detection
```rust
impl TerminalCapabilities {
    pub fn detect() -> Self {
        // Multiplexer detection
        let in_tmux = std::env::var("TMUX").is_ok();
        let in_screen = std::env::var("STY").is_ok();
        let in_zellij = std::env::var("ZELLIJ").is_ok();
        
        // Note: WezTerm/Kitty built-in muxes don't need special handling
        
        Self {
            in_tmux,
            in_screen,
            in_zellij,
            // ...
        }
    }
    
    pub fn in_any_mux(&self) -> bool {
        self.in_tmux || self.in_screen || self.in_zellij
    }
}
```

### tmux Passthrough Wrapper
```rust
/// Wrap sequence for tmux passthrough
/// tmux intercepts escape sequences unless wrapped in DCS passthrough
pub fn tmux_wrap(sequence: &str) -> String {
    if in_tmux() {
        // Double escapes for tmux
        let escaped = sequence.replace('\\x1b', "\\x1b\\x1b");
        format\!("\\x1bPtmux;{}\\x1b\\\\", escaped)
    } else {
        sequence.to_string()
    }
}

// Usage example for OSC 8 hyperlinks under tmux
fn emit_hyperlink(url: &str) -> String {
    let osc = format\!("\\x1b]8;;{}\\x1b\\\\", url);
    tmux_wrap(&osc)
}
```

### Mux-Aware Feature Policies
```rust
impl TerminalCapabilities {
    /// Returns whether sync output should be enabled
    pub fn use_sync_output(&self) -> bool {
        // Disable in multiplexers (passthrough unreliable)
        if self.in_any_mux() {
            return false;
        }
        self.sync_output
    }
    
    /// Returns whether scroll-region optimization is safe
    pub fn use_scroll_region(&self) -> bool {
        // Conservative in muxes due to quirks
        if self.in_any_mux() {
            return false;  // Fall back to overlay redraw
        }
        self.scroll_region
    }
    
    /// OSC 8 hyperlink support
    pub fn use_hyperlinks(&self) -> bool {
        // tmux passthrough needed for hyperlinks
        // Only enable if passthrough is reliable
        if self.in_tmux {
            // Modern tmux (3.3+) supports OSC 8 passthrough
            // but detection is fragile
            return false;  // Conservative default
        }
        self.osc8_hyperlinks
    }
}
```

### Screen Passthrough
```rust
/// screen uses different passthrough syntax
pub fn screen_wrap(sequence: &str) -> String {
    if in_screen() {
        // screen uses P escape sequence
        format\!("\\x1bP{}\\x1b\\\\", sequence)
    } else {
        sequence.to_string()
    }
}
```

### Zellij Handling
```rust
// Zellij generally has better passthrough than tmux/screen
// but some features still need care
impl TerminalCapabilities {
    pub fn zellij_passthrough_mode(&self) -> bool {
        // Zellij 0.39+ has improved passthrough
        self.in_zellij
    }
}
```

## Integration Points

1. **TerminalCapabilities::detect()** - Detect multiplexer presence
2. **Presenter output** - Apply passthrough wrapping where needed
3. **ADR-001 inline mode** - Fall back to overlay redraw in muxes
4. **Feature policies** - Conservative defaults in mux environments

## Tests Required
- [ ] TMUX env var detection
- [ ] STY env var detection (screen)
- [ ] ZELLIJ env var detection
- [ ] tmux_wrap produces correct output
- [ ] screen_wrap produces correct output
- [ ] Feature policies respect mux detection
- [ ] PTY test under tmux (if CI supports)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Passthrough wrappers for tmux/screen

 {"id":"bd-2x0j","title":"Implement property tests for diff correctness and presenter roundtrip","description":"# Implement Property Tests for Diff and Presenter Correctness

## Background
Plan Section 13.1 and J specifically require property tests to ensure diff correctness and presenter output validation. Property tests use randomized inputs to find edge cases humans miss.

## Property Test Categories

### Category 1: Diff Correctness
**Property**: Applying diff changes to old buffer produces new buffer

```rust
proptest! {
    #[test]
    fn diff_apply_produces_target(
        width in 10u16..200,
        height in 5u16..100,
        changes in proptest::collection::vec(
            (0u16..200, 0u16..100, any::<Cell>()), 
            0..1000
        )
    ) {
        // Create old buffer
        let mut old = Buffer::new(width, height);
        
        // Apply random changes to create new buffer
        let mut new = old.clone();
        for (x, y, cell) in &changes {
            if *x < width && *y < height {
                new.set(*x, *y, cell.clone());
            }
        }
        
        // Compute diff
        let diff = BufferDiff::compute(&old, &new);
        
        // Apply diff to old should produce new
        let mut result = old.clone();
        for (x, y) in diff.changes() {
            let cell = new.get(*x, *y).unwrap().clone();
            result.set(*x, *y, cell);
        }
        
        // Verify buffers match
        for y in 0..height {
            for x in 0..width {
                prop_assert_eq!(
                    result.get(x, y),
                    new.get(x, y),
                    "Mismatch at ({}, {})", x, y
                );
            }
        }
    }
}
```

### Category 2: Presenter Roundtrip
**Property**: Presenter output applied to terminal model matches source buffer

```rust
proptest! {
    #[test]
    fn presenter_roundtrip_correct(
        width in 10u16..80,
        height in 5u16..40,
        fill_ratio in 0.0f32..1.0,
    ) {
        // Create random buffer
        let buffer = generate_random_buffer(width, height, fill_ratio);
        
        // Present to bytes
        let mut output = Vec::new();
        let mut presenter = Presenter::new(&mut output, TerminalCapabilities::basic());
        presenter.present_full(&buffer, &GraphemePool::new(), &LinkRegistry::new())
            .unwrap();
        
        // Apply to terminal model
        let mut model = TerminalModel::new(width, height);
        model.apply_output(&output).unwrap();
        
        // Verify match
        prop_assert!(
            model.matches(&buffer, &GraphemePool::new()),
            "Presenter output doesn't reproduce buffer"
        );
    }
}
```

### Category 3: Style State Never Leaks
**Property**: After complete frame, style is reset

```rust
proptest! {
    #[test]
    fn style_state_reset_after_present(
        width in 10u16..80,
        height in 5u16..40,
    ) {
        let buffer = generate_styled_buffer(width, height);
        
        let mut output = Vec::new();
        let mut presenter = Presenter::new(&mut output, TerminalCapabilities::basic());
        presenter.present_full(&buffer, &GraphemePool::new(), &LinkRegistry::new())
            .unwrap();
        
        // Output must end with SGR 0 (reset)
        let output_str = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output);
        prop_assert!(
            output_str.ends_with("\x1b[0m") || 
            output_str.contains("\x1b[0m\x1b["),  // Reset before final positioning
            "Style not reset at end: {}", &output_str[output_str.len().saturating_sub(50)..]
        );
    }
}
```

### Category 4: Links Properly Closed
**Property**: Every OSC 8 link open has corresponding close

```rust
proptest! {
    #[test]
    fn links_balanced(width in 10u16..80, height in 5u16..40) {
        let buffer = generate_buffer_with_links(width, height);
        
        let mut output = Vec::new();
        let mut presenter = Presenter::new(&mut output, TerminalCapabilities { 
            osc8_hyperlinks: true, 
            ..TerminalCapabilities::basic() 
        });
        presenter.present_full(&buffer, &GraphemePool::new(), &links())
            .unwrap();
        
        let output_str = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output);
        
        // Count link opens and closes
        let opens = output_str.matches("\x1b]8;;").count()  // link with URL
            + output_str.matches("\x1b]8;id=").count();     // link with id
        let closes = output_str.matches("\x1b]8;;\x1b\\").count();
        
        // Every open needs a close (or one final close)
        prop_assert!(
            opens <= closes,
            "Unbalanced links: {} opens, {} closes", opens, closes
        );
    }
}
```

### Category 5: Width Invariants
**Property**: Rendering never writes beyond buffer bounds

```rust
proptest! {
    #[test]
    fn render_within_bounds(
        width in 10u16..80,
        height in 5u16..40,
        text in ".*",
    ) {
        let mut buffer = Buffer::new(width, height);
        
        // Render text into buffer (via whatever rendering API)
        render_text(&mut buffer, &text, 0, 0, width);
        
        // Check no writes beyond bounds
        // (Buffer should enforce this internally, but verify)
        for y in 0..height {
            let mut x = 0;
            while x < width {
                let cell = buffer.get(x, y).unwrap();
                let cell_width = cell.width() as u16;
                
                // Wide char can't overflow right edge
                prop_assert!(
                    x + cell_width <= width,
                    "Wide char at ({}, {}) overflows (width {})",
                    x, y, cell_width
                );
                
                x += cell_width.max(1);
            }
        }
    }
}
```

- `generate_styled_buffer(w, h)` - Random styles
- `generate_buffer_with_links(w, h)` - Random hyperlinks

-{"id":"bd-2yu","title":"Implement run grouping and style-run coalescing in Presenter","description":"# Implement Run Grouping and Style-Run Coalescing in Presenter

## Background
ADR-002 and Plan Section 8.1 specify that the Presenter must emit "grouped runs" rather than per-cell cursor moves. This is critical for minimal ANSI output and flicker reduction.

## Why This Matters
Without run grouping:
- Each changed cell requires a cursor move: `\x1b[y;xH`
- Each cell change might reset style: `\x1b[0m...`
- Output is O(changes × escape_overhead)
- Slow terminals show visible tearing

With run grouping:
- Consecutive changed cells emit one cursor move
- Style only changes when cell style differs
- Output is O(runs × escape_overhead)
- Smooth, fast rendering

## Technical Design

### ChangeRun Structure
```rust
/// A contiguous run of changed cells on a single row
pub struct ChangeRun {
    pub y: u16,   // Row
    pub x0: u16,  // Start column (inclusive)
    pub x1: u16,  // End column (inclusive)
}
```

### Run Grouping Algorithm
```rust
impl BufferDiff {
    /// Convert point changes into row-major runs
    pub fn runs(&self, width: u16) -> Vec<ChangeRun> {
        if self.changes.is_empty() {
            return Vec::new();
        }
        
        // Sort by (y, x) for row-major order
        let mut sorted: Vec<_> = self.changes.iter().copied().collect();
        sorted.sort_by_key(|(x, y)| (*y, *x));
        
        let mut runs = Vec::new();
        let mut i = 0;
        
        while i < sorted.len() {
            let (x0, y) = sorted[i];
            let mut x1 = x0;
            i += 1;
            
            // Coalesce consecutive x positions on same row
            while i < sorted.len() {
                let (x, yy) = sorted[i];
                if yy != y || x != x1 + 1 {
                    break;
                }
                x1 = x;
                i += 1;
            }
            
            runs.push(ChangeRun { y, x0, x1 });
        }
        
        runs
    }
}
```

### Style-Run Coalescing
Within a run, group cells by style to minimize SGR changes:

```rust
impl Presenter {
    fn emit_run(
        &mut self,
        run: &ChangeRun,
        buffer: &Buffer,
        pool: &GraphemePool,
        links: &LinkRegistry,
    ) -> io::Result<()> {
        // Single cursor move for entire run
        self.move_cursor_to(run.x0, run.y)?;
        
        // Emit cells, tracking style changes
        for x in run.x0..=run.x1 {
            let cell = buffer.get_unchecked(x, run.y);
            if cell.is_continuation() {
                continue;  // Skip wide char continuations
            }
            
            // Only emit style change if different from current
            self.emit_style_if_changed(cell)?;
            self.emit_link_if_changed(cell, links)?;
            self.emit_content(cell, pool)?;
            
            // Cursor advances naturally after character output
            self.cursor_x += cell.width() as u16;
        }
        
        Ok(())
    }
    
    fn emit_style_if_changed(&mut self, cell: &Cell) -> io::Result<()> {
        let new_style = CellStyle {
            fg: cell.fg,
            bg: cell.bg,
            attrs: cell.attrs.flags(),
        };
        
        if Some(new_style) == self.current_style {
            return Ok(());  // No change needed
        }
        
        // Emit minimal SGR change
        // v1: reset+apply (simple, correct)
        // future: incremental (fewer bytes)
        self.writer.write_all(b"\x1b[0")?;
        // ... emit style codes ...
        self.writer.write_all(b"m")?;
        self.current_style = Some(new_style);
        
        Ok(())
    }
```

4. **Empty cell optimization** - Runs of spaces can use `\x1b[nX` (erase characters)

+{"id":"bd-2yu","title":"Implement run grouping and style-run coalescing in Presenter","description":"# Implement Run Grouping and Style-Run Coalescing in Presenter

## Background
ADR-002 and Plan Section 8.1 specify that the Presenter must emit "grouped runs" rather than per-cell cursor moves. This is critical for minimal ANSI output and flicker reduction.

## Why This Matters
Without run grouping:
- Each changed cell requires a cursor move: `\x1b[y;xH`
- Each cell change might reset style: `\x1b[0m...`
- Output is O(changes × escape_overhead)
- Slow terminals show visible tearing

With run grouping:
- Consecutive changed cells emit one cursor move
- Style only changes when cell style differs
- Output is O(runs × escape_overhead)
- Smooth, fast rendering

## Technical Design

### ChangeRun Structure
```rust
/// A contiguous run of changed cells on a single row
pub struct ChangeRun {
    pub y: u16,   // Row
    pub x0: u16,  // Start column (inclusive)
    pub x1: u16,  // End column (inclusive)
}
```

### Run Grouping Algorithm
```rust
impl BufferDiff {
    /// Convert point changes into row-major runs
    pub fn runs(&self, width: u16) -> Vec<ChangeRun> {
        if self.changes.is_empty() {
            return Vec::new();
        }
        
        // Sort by (y, x) for row-major order
        let mut sorted: Vec<_> = self.changes.iter().copied().collect();
        sorted.sort_by_key(|(x, y)| (*y, *x));
        
        let mut runs = Vec::new();
        let mut i = 0;
        
        while i < sorted.len() {
            let (x0, y) = sorted[i];
            let mut x1 = x0;
            i += 1;
            
            // Coalesce consecutive x positions on same row
            while i < sorted.len() {
                let (x, yy) = sorted[i];
                if yy != y || x != x1 + 1 {
                    break;
                }
                x1 = x;
                i += 1;
            }
            
            runs.push(ChangeRun { y, x0, x1 });
        }
        
        runs
    }
}
```

### Style-Run Coalescing
Within a run, group cells by style to minimize SGR changes:

```rust
impl Presenter {
    fn emit_run(
        &mut self,
        run: &ChangeRun,
        buffer: &Buffer,
        pool: &GraphemePool,
        links: &LinkRegistry,
    ) -> io::Result<()> {
        // Single cursor move for entire run
        self.move_cursor_to(run.x0, run.y)?;
        
        // Emit cells, tracking style changes
        for x in run.x0..=run.x1 {
            let cell = buffer.get_unchecked(x, run.y);
            if cell.is_continuation() {
                continue;  // Skip wide char continuations
            }
            
            // Only emit style change if different from current
            self.emit_style_if_changed(cell)?;
            self.emit_link_if_changed(cell, links)?;
            self.emit_content(cell, pool)?;
            
            // Cursor advances naturally after character output
            self.cursor_x += cell.width() as u16;
        }
        
        Ok(())
    }
    
    fn emit_style_if_changed(&mut self, cell: &Cell) -> io::Result<()> {
        let new_style = CellStyle {
            fg: cell.fg,
            bg: cell.bg,
            attrs: cell.attrs.flags(),
        };
        
        if Some(new_style) == self.current_style {
            return Ok(());  // No change needed
        }
        
        // Emit minimal SGR change
        // v1: reset+apply (simple, correct)
        // future: incremental (fewer bytes)
        self.writer.write_all(b"\x1b[0")?;
        // ... emit style codes ...
        self.writer.write_all(b"m")?;
        self.current_style = Some(new_style);
        
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

### Optimization Opportunities
1. **Skip cursor move when at expected position** - After emitting a run, cursor may be at next change
2. **Batch style attributes** - Emit `\x1b[0;1;38;2;r;g;bm` not separate sequences
3. **Link run grouping** - Keep link open across cells with same link_id
4. **Empty cell optimization** - Runs of spaces can use `\x1b[nX` (erase characters)

## Performance Targets
- Cursor moves reduced by 50-90% vs naive
- Style changes reduced by 30-70%
- Bytes per frame for "small changes" is O(changes), not O(screen)

- [ ] Empty diff produces no runs

- [ ] Consecutive cells on row produce one run
- [ ] Non-consecutive cells produce multiple runs

 {"id":"bd-2zd","title":"Implement scroll-region optimization for inline mode","description":"PHASE 2.5 - SCROLL REGION OPTIMIZATION

## Background
Scroll regions (DECSTBM) can optimize inline mode by pinning the UI region and letting logs scroll naturally. This is an OPTIMIZATION, not the baseline.

## Technical Requirements

### DECSTBM Usage
- Set scroll region to exclude UI: ESC[1;{log_bottom}r
- Logs scroll within region, UI stays pinned
- Must restore scroll region on exit

### Capability Gating
Only use when:
- caps.scroll_region == true
- NOT in tmux/screen/zellij (mux passthrough issues)
- Terminal known to support correctly

### Implementation

pub struct ScrollRegionPresenter {
    ui_height: u16,
    scroll_region_active: bool,
}

impl ScrollRegionPresenter {
    pub fn setup_scroll_region(&mut self, writer: &mut TerminalWriter) {
        if !self.can_use_scroll_region() {
            return;  // Fall back to overlay
        }
        
        let (_, height) = writer.size();
        let log_bottom = height - self.ui_height;
        
        // Set scroll region (excludes UI at bottom)
        write!(writer, "\x1b[1;{}r", log_bottom)?;
        self.scroll_region_active = true;
    }
    
    pub fn restore_scroll_region(&mut self, writer: &mut TerminalWriter) {
        if self.scroll_region_active {
            writer.write_all(b"\x1b[r")?;  // Reset to full screen
            self.scroll_region_active = false;
        }
    }
    
    fn can_use_scroll_region(&self) -> bool {
        let caps = TerminalCapabilities::detect();
        caps.scroll_region && 
        !caps.in_tmux &&
        !caps.in_screen &&
        !caps.in_zellij
    }
}

### Mux Detection
- TMUX env var presence
- TERM containing "screen"
- ZELLIJ env var presence

### Resize Handling
On terminal resize:
1. Disable scroll region
2. Recalculate dimensions
3. Re-enable if still appropriate

## Tests Required
- [ ] Scroll region setup/teardown
- [ ] Logs scroll within region
- [ ] UI stays pinned at bottom
- [ ] Mux detection works
- [ ] Resize re-anchoring
- [ ] PTY test: scroll region behavior

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] DECSTBM used correctly
- [ ] Capability gating works
- [ ] Mux passthrough disabled
- [ ] Fallback to overlay works
- [ ] Clean restore on exit

 {"id":"bd-6qxh","title":"Frame Caching with Smart Invalidation","description":"# Frame Caching with Smart Invalidation

## Background & Motivation

Many widgets are static most of the time:
- Headers and footers
- Borders and decorations
- Rarely-changing status bars

Re-rendering them every frame wastes CPU.

**The solution:**
- Cache rendered widget output
- Invalidate on model change or resize
- Composite cached content with live content

**Relationship to existing beads:**
- Requires bd-10i.3.6 (Buffer with scissor/opacity stacks)
- Complements bd-1iqn (Render Budget)

## Technical Design

```rust
pub struct CachedWidget<W: Widget> {
    inner: W,
    cache: Option<CachedBuffer>,
    last_area: Option<Rect>,
    dirty: bool,
}

pub struct CachedBuffer {
    buffer: Buffer,
    hash: u64,  // Content hash for change detection
}

impl<W: Widget> CachedWidget<W> {
    pub fn new(widget: W) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn mark_dirty(&mut self) { self.dirty = true; }
    
    pub fn render(&mut self, area: Rect, buf: &mut Buffer) {
        let needs_render = self.dirty 
            || self.last_area != Some(area)
            || self.cache.is_none();
            
        if needs_render {
            let mut cache_buf = Buffer::empty(area);
            self.inner.render(area, &mut cache_buf);
            self.cache = Some(CachedBuffer::new(cache_buf));
            self.last_area = Some(area);
            self.dirty = false;
        }
        
        if let Some(cache) = &self.cache {
            buf.merge(&cache.buffer);
        }
    }
}
```

## Logging Requirements

```rust
// Cache hit (TRACE level)
tracing::trace!(widget = name, "Cache hit, using cached buffer");

// Cache miss (DEBUG level)
tracing::debug!(widget = name, reason = ?reason, "Cache miss, re-rendering");

// Cache invalidated (DEBUG level)
tracing::debug!(widget = name, "Cache invalidated via mark_dirty()");
```

## E2E Tests

```bash
# Test cache hit rate
./ftui-harness --test-mode cache-stats --duration 10s --static-widgets 5
# Assert: cache hit rate > 95%

# Test memory bounds
./ftui-harness --test-mode cache-memory --cached-widgets 100
# Assert: memory < 10MB for 100 widgets

# Verify correctness
./ftui-harness --test-mode cache-verify --compare-to-uncached
# Assert: output identical
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CachedWidget<W> wrapper implemented
- [ ] Caching works for static widgets
- [ ] Invalidation on mark_dirty()
- [ ] Resize triggers re-render
- [ ] Memory usage bounded
- [ ] Content hash detects changes
- [ ] Integration with widget tree
- [ ] No stale content ever displayed
- [ ] All tests pass","notes":"## Cache Safety Guidelines

### When to Use CachedWidget
- Static content (headers, footers, borders)
- Content that changes infrequently
- Expensive rendering operations

### When NOT to Use CachedWidget
- Dynamic content (logs, timers, animations)
- Content that depends on external state
- Small/fast widgets (caching overhead > render cost)

### Cache Coherence
The cache can become stale if:
1. Widget props change without mark_dirty()
2. External state affects rendering
3. Theme/style changes globally

Solution: Use automatic dirty detection via content hash:
```rust
impl<W: Widget + Hash> CachedWidget<W> {
    fn render(&mut self, area: Rect, buf: &mut Buffer) {
        let current_hash = self.inner.content_hash();
        
        let needs_render = self.dirty
            || self.last_area != Some(area)
            || self.cache.as_ref().map(|c| c.hash) != Some(current_hash);
        
        // ...
    }
}
```

### Memory Management
```rust
impl<W: Widget> CachedWidget<W> {
    /// Drop cache to free memory
    pub fn clear_cache(&mut self) {
        self.cache = None;
    }
    
    /// Check cache memory usage
    pub fn cache_size(&self) -> usize {
        self.cache.as_ref().map(|c| c.buffer.memory_size()).unwrap_or(0)
    }
}
```

 {"id":"bd-7u8","title":"Implement cursor save/restore strategy (DEC vs ANSI vs emulated)","description":"# Implement Cursor Save/Restore Strategy

## Background
Plan Section 9.3 describes a critical cursor save/restore strategy for inline mode robustness. Terminals differ in which save/restore sequences they support reliably.

## Problem Statement
Inline mode requires saving cursor position before drawing UI and restoring after. However:
- DEC save/restore (\`ESC 7\` / \`ESC 8\`) is preferred but not universal
- ANSI save/restore (\`CSI s\` / \`CSI u\`) is an alternative
- Some terminals/muxes have quirks with either
- Must handle failures gracefully

## Layered Strategy (from Plan)

### Layer 1: DEC Save/Restore (Preferred)
\`\`\`rust
const DEC_SAVE: &[u8] = b"\x1b7";    // ESC 7 (DECSC)
const DEC_RESTORE: &[u8] = b"\x1b8"; // ESC 8 (DECRC)
\`\`\`
- Most widely supported on modern terminals
- Saves more state (cursor position, attributes, charset)
- Works in tmux/screen with passthrough

### Layer 2: ANSI Save/Restore (Fallback)
\`\`\`rust
const ANSI_SAVE: &[u8] = b"\x1b[s";    // CSI s
const ANSI_RESTORE: &[u8] = b"\x1b[u"; // CSI u
\`\`\`
- Less widely supported
- Only saves cursor position (not attributes)
- May conflict with some terminal modes

### Layer 3: Emulated (Last Resort)
\`\`\`rust
// Track cursor position explicitly
struct CursorTracker {
    x: u16,
    y: u16,
}

impl CursorTracker {
    fn save(&mut self, current_x: u16, current_y: u16) {
        self.x = current_x;
        self.y = current_y;
    }
    
    fn restore(&self, writer: &mut impl Write) -> io::Result<()> {
        write!(writer, "\x1b[{};{}H", self.y + 1, self.x + 1)
    }
}
\`\`\`
- Works everywhere that supports CUP
- Requires tracking cursor position throughout
- More overhead but guaranteed to work

## Strategy Selection
\`\`\`rust
pub enum CursorSaveStrategy {
    Dec,       // ESC 7 / ESC 8
    Ansi,      // CSI s / CSI u
    Emulated,  // Track and CUP
    Auto,      // Detect best for environment
}

impl CursorSaveStrategy {
    pub fn detect(caps: &TerminalCapabilities) -> Self {
        // Most terminals support DEC
        if !caps.in_screen {
            return Self::Dec;
        }
        // Screen has quirks, try ANSI
        Self::Ansi
    }
}
\`\`\`

## Implementation
\`\`\`rust
pub struct CursorManager {
    strategy: CursorSaveStrategy,
    saved_position: Option<(u16, u16)>,  // For emulated mode
}

impl CursorManager {
    pub fn save(&mut self, writer: &mut impl Write, current_pos: (u16, u16)) -> io::Result<()> {
        match self.strategy {
            CursorSaveStrategy::Dec => writer.write_all(DEC_SAVE),
            CursorSaveStrategy::Ansi => writer.write_all(ANSI_SAVE),
            CursorSaveStrategy::Emulated => {
                self.saved_position = Some(current_pos);
                Ok(())
            }
            CursorSaveStrategy::Auto => {
                // Try DEC, it's most common
                writer.write_all(DEC_SAVE)
            }
        }
    }
    
    pub fn restore(&mut self, writer: &mut impl Write) -> io::Result<()> {
        match self.strategy {
            CursorSaveStrategy::Dec => writer.write_all(DEC_RESTORE),
            CursorSaveStrategy::Ansi => writer.write_all(ANSI_RESTORE),
            CursorSaveStrategy::Emulated => {
                if let Some((x, y)) = self.saved_position {
                    write!(writer, "\x1b[{};{}H", y + 1, x + 1)?;
                }
                Ok(())
            }
            CursorSaveStrategy::Auto => writer.write_all(DEC_RESTORE),
        }
    }
}
\`\`\`

## PTY Test: Cursor Restoration
Test that verifies cursor is correctly restored:
1. Move cursor to known position
2. Save cursor
3. Move cursor elsewhere
4. Restore cursor
5. Query position (DSR) and verify matches saved position

## Tests Required
- [ ] DEC save/restore works on xterm-like terminals
- [ ] ANSI save/restore works as fallback
- [ ] Emulated mode tracks and restores correctly
- [ ] Auto-detection chooses appropriate strategy
- [ ] PTY test verifies cursor contract per strategy

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CursorManager implemented with all three strategies
- [ ] Auto-detection based on TerminalCapabilities
- [ ] Integrated into InlinePresenter
- [ ] PTY tests pass for all strategies
- [ ] Documented which terminals use which strategy
